tweets.text:
John Kim on how we misprice risk:

"No one actually invests with a lot of risk.

There are only people who perceive there's no risk. 

People convince themselves that the risk is far less than what it really is so they can receive the reward. 

If somebody says, "I'm risk-loving." No you're not. You're just really good at rationalizing away the risk. 

If you know your outcome is gigantic, you just have to rationalize the risk to half of what it really is, and you'll say yes. 

That's where a lot of cognitive mistakes are made in investing."
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
John Kim has raised over $70 billion in his 30-year career.

At General Catalyst, he raised several flagship funds and helped build the firm into one of the largest venture investors in the world.

Now he leads fundraising at Lila Sciences, a company building scientific https://t.co/SO9gVBniVE
tweets.full_text:
John Kim on how we misprice risk:

"No one actually invests with a lot of risk.

There are only people who perceive there's no risk. 

People convince themselves that the risk is far less than what it really is so they can receive the reward. 

If somebody says, "I'm risk-loving." No you're not. You're just really good at rationalizing away the risk. 

If you know your outcome is gigantic, you just have to rationalize the risk to half of what it really is, and you'll say yes. 

That's where a lot of cognitive mistakes are made in investing."

tweets.text: You're either a power or not, there is no "middle."

thread_tweets.text: You're either a power or not, there is no "middle."
thread_tweets.full_text: You're either a power or not, there is no "middle."

thread_tweets.text: I don't like calling Iran a middle power, both because its military instruments are real and because it operates outside the US-anchored system that such rankings assume + it's a state with a "cause". Raymond Aron's classification serves better, dividing states into homogeneous and heterogeneous camps and sorting them as ordering states, protected states, and denial states.
thread_tweets.full_text: I don't like calling Iran a middle power, both because its military instruments are real and because it operates outside the US-anchored system that such rankings assume + it's a state with a "cause". Raymond Aron's classification serves better, dividing states into homogeneous and heterogeneous camps and sorting them as ordering states, protected states, and denial states.

tweets.full_text:
You're either a power or not, there is no "middle."

I don't like calling Iran a middle power, both because its military instruments are real and because it operates outside the US-anchored system that such rankings assume + it's a state with a "cause". Raymond Aron's classification serves better, dividing states into homogeneous and heterogeneous camps and sorting them as ordering states, protected states, and denial states.

tweets.text: Elbridge's point is simple. Alignment with the United States, which commands the resources, the arms, and the will to defend its interests, pays. Hedging that exposes supply chains and infrastructure to China does not.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
There is a great deal of hubbub about a collective “middle powers”strategy these days. 

At DoW, we are not concerned that this is a serious possibility. Rather, we are more concerned that a few allies and partners will *think it is* and waste valuable time, money, and political
tweets.full_text: Elbridge's point is simple. Alignment with the United States, which commands the resources, the arms, and the will to defend its interests, pays. Hedging that exposes supply chains and infrastructure to China does not.

Last night it was "next week we're gonna knock out all their power plants and bridges."

This morning it was "Iran called and wants to make a deal".

Market didn't bat an eye at either.

tweets.text:
It's been a good year, and for the most part we cooked

avoided all the drawdown in crypto this year, and had been long hype + adding a lot on the way up as the core exposure
 
Got a good amount of the AI trade in stonks and general stock exposure for the past 18 months 

longed eth at 1766. not crazy size tho

Also, just some general thoughts on this year below: 

The average person would be better off trying to capture the meat of the move. vs trying to time bottoms. How many times did people call a macro bottom in the past year? it felt like everyday. 

Let's keep this idea simple, Im going to oversimplify it but as a framework. lets say instead of catching a bottom. You pay a 20% premium from the lows to enter but you structurally get a reclaim. The first HH. You can then ride that up bc your invalidation is so so so so cheap at that point and so is the entry. You know the spot it can't lose structurally. if it's the start of an uptrend, you have all the upside without the time cost, opp costs, emotional costs, and very real drawdowns. And instead get to ride momentum from the reclaims. 

Lets say $100 is that HH point, buying at $99 can be extremely expensive but buying at $101 can be very cheap. Bc of market structure + trend. The 99 can just be trend continuation and the next LH, while the 101 is a restest of a major reclaim that leads to continuation but also gives you a cheap exit if wrong.

thread_tweets.text:
i think the market structure mental framework that shows lower prices aren't always a great deal is a good way to think and trade markets in general. bc it comes at a greater cost more often than not. 

the trend is truly your friend.

tweets.text:
I see none of you recall Hochuls previous data center moratorium - targeted at one bitcoin mining facility in upstate NY. A very amusing episode 

- was inspired by a mass hysteria campaign of “fossil fuel plants coming online to mine bitcoin” (nonexistent)
- didn’t get the one Bitcoin mining facility it targeted to shut down 
- that one Bitcoin miner/power plant was straightforwardly useful to NYISO in terms of providing backup natgas generation - sorely needed after Indian Point shutdown 
- no evidence it stopped the construction of any bitcoin mining facility at all 
- the feared wave of “fossil fuel plant reenergization” via Bitcoin never materialized - anywhere 
- achieved nothing

tweets.text:
#Uranium #Nuclear #Athabasca #Mining ⚛️

Russell Lake is shaping up to be one of the most compelling exploration stories in the Athabasca Basin.

Skyharbour Resources $SYH / $SYH.V controls a 73,000+ hectare district-scale project strategically located between Cameco's McArthur River Mine and Key Lake Mill and adjacent to Denison's Wheeler River Project. The project also benefits from road access, power infrastructure, a fully permitted exploration camp, and over 95,000 metres of historical drilling that has generated numerous high-priority targets.

The partnership with Denison Mines adds significant technical expertise, funding and validation, while 15,000+ metres of drilling planned in 2026 across multiple target areas provides a strong pipeline of potential catalysts.

Skyharbour continues to build one of the strongest exploration portfolios in the world's premier uranium district.

Disclosure: Paid partnership with Skyharbour Resources. Not financial advice. Do your own research.

tweets.text:
With markets this choppy, investing in protocols with revenue + buybacks can be a sound bet.

But tbh a lot of buyback proposals sound big and don't really move the needle. The percentages are small, and teams quietly drop them when they change their mind.

Jito's JIP-38 caught my eye though:

• This isn't a small slice. 80% of all JTX fees get bought and burned. The other 20% goes back into building the platform.

• Everything bought gets burned on-chain. It's not parked in some treasury where it can leak back out

• They can't redirect the money without a separate governance vote. Meaning it's hard to be sneaky.

Worth keeping in mind that JTX hasn't launched yet. So, it's easy to commit 100% of revenue that doesn't exist. And the buyback period is only for one year (so far).

Whether this moves the needle depends on JTX actually getting some traction.

And the part I think people will miss is that in Q4 2027, ALL the fee streams (JitoSOL, BAM, Block Engine) go up for a vote on whether they get routed the same way.

JTX is basically the test run and maybe real money comes later.

Jito's price has gone up 152% since it's February lows. This could be the catalyst to take it even further imo.

What do you think?
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
JIP-38 is now live.

Value should live with the Network. This proposal formally establishes Jito as a token-centric network, committing 100% of the Jito DAO's revenue share from @JTX_trade to programmatic buyback and burns of $JTO for at least 1 year from JTX launch.

tweets.text:
Everyone’s talking about how @RobinhoodCrypto revenue has flipped @base.

There’s a real signal that’s 20x smaller and way more important.

First, the revenue’s impressive. The 2 week old chain is earning more daily revenue than Base with just 4% of its TVL, and it's already doing 70% of Base's DEX volume.

But where’s the Robinhood activity coming from?

Over 80% of Robinhood's TVL sits in lending vaults like Morpho and Steakhouse, and a chunk of the trading spike is from memecoins.

The most interesting part to me is tokenized assets. Both chains are mostly stablecoins, but look at the tokenized stocks:

• Base: ~$5B in tokenized assets, and only 0.2% is stocks. About $10M.
• Robinhood: ~$239M in tokenized assets, but 4.8% is stocks. About $11-12M.

So a chain with 4% of Base's TVL is already holding more tokenized stock value than Base itself.

It makes sense because Robinhood built its reputation from trading stocks. But I’m surprised at how fast the growth is.

What I'm watching is whether the volume holds once the hype fades, or whether tokenized stocks end up being the real story here.

tweets.text:
$AMZN, $META, and $GOOG are finally waking up to the fact that they’ve been the suckers of Wall Street, while $SNDK, $MU, $AMD, and $INTC, which were considered by almost everybody on Wall Street to be the easiest consensus longs because of the expected memory shortage over the next three years, are being taken out to the woodshed this week.

If a trade looks obvious, easy, and everyone agrees on it, be very careful. When everyone is on the same side of the boat, it usually doesn’t take much to tip it over.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
HyperScaler Prisoner’s Dilemma

The AI spending race is starting to look like a classic Prisoner’s Dilemma.

$MSFT, $META, $AMZN, and $GOOGL would probably all benefit if they collectively slowed capex growth and focused on profits. But nobody wants to be the first to blink and

thread_tweets.text:
Hey Nash, thanks for sharing your expert opinion. I really appreciate it.

I noticed you’re based in Boston, so I was just wondering: which part of the Israeli government do you work for? Or are you advising the Prime Minister’s office? Maybe the Ministry of Finance? Or do you just have a direct line to the cabinet that the rest of us don’t know about?

I’m only asking because you seem remarkably confident that your opinion outweighs the views of the parties actually involved. Thought you might have some inside access the rest of us are missing.
thread_tweets.full_text:
Hey Nash, thanks for sharing your expert opinion. I really appreciate it.

I noticed you’re based in Boston, so I was just wondering: which part of the Israeli government do you work for? Or are you advising the Prime Minister’s office? Maybe the Ministry of Finance? Or do you just have a direct line to the cabinet that the rest of us don’t know about?

I’m only asking because you seem remarkably confident that your opinion outweighs the views of the parties actually involved. Thought you might have some inside access the rest of us are missing.

thread_tweets.text:
$ZIM does not have debt. It has long term vessel leases, and those are very different.

A simple analogy: imagine I rent a taxi for 14 years at $10,000 per year. I use that taxi to generate $15,000 per year in income. You wouldn’t call that lease “debt.” It’s a productive asset that generates cash.

Now take it one step further. Most of ZIM’s vessel leases were signed several years ago, before today’s shortage of modern container ships. Because supply is now much tighter, those lease contracts have become far more valuable than when they were signed.

Going back to the taxi example, imagine you locked in a 14 year lease at $10,000 per year. A few years later, taxi production collapses and there aren’t enough taxis available. New leases now cost $15,000 per year. Suddenly, your lease is an asset. It has economic value because you secured it at a price well below today’s market rate.

That is similar to ZIM’s position. Many of its long term vessel leases were signed at rates that are now significantly below current market levels, making those contracts valuable economic assets rather than liabilities.

tweets.text:
The biggest problem humanity faces is that we're running out of people.

Sounds insane, I know. Fewer people means fewer mouths to feed, more resources to go around, and less pressure on the planet. All true, until you follow it through.

GDP is driven by demographics. A smaller workforce means a lower ceiling on output, and we're already carrying more debt than we can service.

So governments are left with one question. How do you grow when there's no one left to do the work? There's only one answer. Robots and AI.

That's why the Exponential Age is arriving faster than anyone forecast, not through choice but through necessity.

tweets.text: @erwblo @CJwiseB Dit is een onderdeel van het Cantillon effect, waardoor het grootste deel van het bijgedrukte geld in activa als aandelen en real estate terecht komt. Het komt uiteindelijk allemaal door de overheid die maar geld blijft printen, ipv de overheidsuitgaven te verlagen.

tweets.text:
Controversial opinion:  

Conflating "ETH is money" with network congestion through EIP-1559's token burn created misaligned incentives.   

The burn was treated as evidence of good monetary policy, even when it came from failing to scale the network.   

In the end, congestion pushed projects and volume elsewhere, eventually reducing the burn itself.
